description: C64 video chip. Sprites, scrolling, raster tricks. Demo scene legend.

name: Amiga OCS Agnus 8361
description: Original Amiga DMA controller. Copper + Blitter. OCS chipset.

name: Amiga AGA Alice
description: A4000/A1200 DMA controller. AGA chipset. Commodore's last stand.

name: Commodore Amiga Denise 8362
description: Original Amiga display chip. 32 colors from 4096. HAM mode.

name: Sony PS1 Geometry Transform Engine
description: PS1 geometry coprocessor. 1.5M flat-shaded polys/sec. 3D revolution.

name: Loongson 3A5000
description: China's homegrown LoongArch CPU. Not x86, not ARM — its own thing. Digital sovereignty in silicon.

name: Loongson 3A6000
description: The sequel. Quad-issue out-of-order, trades blows with a 10th-gen Core i3. The Great Firewall's proudest son.

name: Zhaoxin KaiXian KX-6640MA
description: Chinese x86 clone via VIA's Centaur lineage. The bloodline lives on in the most unexpected place.

name: Zhaoxin KaiSheng KH-40000
description: Server-class Chinese x86. Trying to replace Xeons in government offices one bureaucrat at a time.

name: Phytium FT-2000/4
description: ARM-based Chinese server chip. Entity-listed by the US, which is basically a badge of honor.

name: Phytium D2000
description: Desktop-class ARM from Phytium. Powers the Huawei Qingyun desktops. Sanction-proof computing.

name: HiSilicon Kirin 9000
description: Huawei's magnum opus. 5nm before the sanctions hammer fell. The last of its kind — a silicon swan song.

name: HiSilicon Kirin 710
description: Mid-range Kirin for the masses. Powered a billion budget Huaweis before the world changed.

name: Sunway SW26010
description: 260 cores of Chinese supercomputing fury. Powered TaihuLight to #1 on TOP500. No Western IP inside.

name: SMIC Black Market Relabel
description: Nobody knows what this actually is. Found with sanded-off markings at a Shenzhen flea market. Could be anything. Could be everything.

name: Elbrus-8S
description: Russian VLIW processor. Explicitly designed so Russia doesn't need Western CPUs. Mission... partially accomplished.

name: Elbrus-16S
description: 16-core VLIW beast from Mother Russia. The compiler has to be smarter than the chip. That's the whole point.

name: Baikal-T1
description: MIPS-based Russian CPU for networking gear. Named after the deepest lake on Earth. Not that deep architecturally.

name: Baikal-M
description: ARM-based Russian desktop chip. Cortex-A57 cores in 2021 — vintage ARM, freshly packaged.

name: MCST R1000
description: Elbrus architecture shrunk for embedded use. When your missile guidance needs domestic silicon.

name: KM1810VM86
description: Soviet 8086 clone. Reverse-engineered with love and five-year plans. Pin-compatible if you squint.

name: K1801VM1
description: Soviet PDP-11 clone. Powered BK home computers and taught a generation of Russian hackers BASIC.

name: K580VM80A
description: Soviet 8080 clone. The Iron Curtain couldn't stop good chip designs from being... borrowed.

name: NexGen Nx586
description: The x86 clone that needed its own chipset. AMD bought NexGen and turned it into the K6. Ugly duckling origin story.

name: Cyrix MII
description: Cyrix's last stand. PR-rated to mislead, FPU bad enough to cry about. Budget Quake was a slideshow.

name: Transmeta Efficeon TM8800
description: Code morphing v2. Could pretend to be x86 even harder. Still couldn't pretend to be profitable.

name: VIA C7
description: Last of the Centaur x86 bloodline. Built-in hardware RNG and AES. The spy's budget laptop chip.

name: VIA Nano
description: VIA's out-of-order 64-bit swan song. Actually decent. Too little, too late. RIP Centaur Technology.

name: IDT WinChip C6
description: Socket 7 x86 clone from Integrated Device Technology. Cheap, simple, slow. The K-Mart of processors.

name: IDT WinChip 2
description: Added 3DNow! and MMX. Still couldn't win. Centaur got bought by VIA and the name died here.

name: UMC U5S (Green CPU)
description: Unlicensed 486 clone from Taiwan. Intel sued. UMC lost. Every surviving chip is a contraband artifact.

name: SiS 550
description: SiS tried making a CPU once. Rise mP6 core in disguise with integrated graphics. We don't talk about this.

name: DM&P Vortex86DX
description: Taiwanese x86 SoC for embedded systems. In every karaoke machine and industrial robot you've never noticed.

name: INMOS Transputer T9000
description: The Transputer sequel that was too ambitious. Virtual channels, superscalar — and catastrophically late. RIP INMOS.

name: Acorn ARM1
description: THE FIRST ARM CHIP EVER. 25,000 transistors designed by a tiny team. Now its descendants run the entire planet.

name: Acorn ARM2
description: Powered the Archimedes. Hardware multiply added. Still only 30K transistors — efficiency incarnate.

name: Fairchild CLIPPER C100
description: Early RISC contender that lost to SPARC and MIPS. Intergraph used it until they didn't. A cautionary footnote.

name: National Semiconductor NS32032
description: The 'NS32K' 32-bit family that was supposed to rival 68000. Buggy, slow, forgotten. Used in some early workstations.

name: Zilog Z80000
description: Zilog's 32-bit follow-up to the legendary Z80. Arrived too late to matter. The Z80 lived on; this didn't.

name: PA-RISC 8600
description: HP's late-era PA-RISC. Massive cache, great FP. Then Itanium came along and HP-UX wept.

name: DEC Alpha 21364 (EV7)
description: THE LAST ALPHA EVER MADE. Integrated memory controller before AMD made it cool. A legend's final breath.

name: SGI MIPS R14000
description: Late-era SGI MIPS. Powered Onyx visualizers and Origin servers. Hollywood rendering on IRIX one last time.

name: Sun UltraSPARC T2
description: 8 cores, 64 threads in 2007. CoolThreads marketing wasn't wrong — it was ahead of its time and also Sun's last hurrah.

name: Hitachi SuperH SH-4
description: Dreamcast's brain. Superscalar RISC with hardware geometry engine. The console that died too young.

name: NEC V60
description: NEC's attempt at 32-bit supremacy. Not quite CISC, not quite RISC. Found in obscure Japanese workstations.

name: NEC V850
description: Embedded RISC for automotive and industrial. Probably in your car's ECU right now, quietly judging your driving.

name: Fujitsu SPARC64 XII
description: One of the last SPARC chips ever made. 4.25 GHz, because Fujitsu didn't know the meaning of quit.

name: IBM z15
description: Mainframe chip running at 5.2 GHz. Banks, airlines, and governments depend on this. It will outlive us all.

name: SiFive P670
description: High-performance RISC-V application core. Vector extensions included. The open-source ISA grows teeth.

name: Alibaba T-Head XuanTie C910
description: Alibaba's RISC-V core. Open-sourced by a trillion-dollar company. The weirdest timeline for open silicon.

name: StarFive JH7110
description: Powers the VisionFive 2 SBC. RISC-V Linux desktop for $50. The Raspberry Pi of the open ISA revolution.

name: Kendryte K210
description: Tiny RISC-V with a neural network accelerator. AI at the edge before it was cool. Sipeed boards everywhere.

name: Espressif ESP32-C3
description: RISC-V WiFi+BLE MCU for $1. In every smart plug, fairy light, and IoT horror device. Espressif chose violence.

name: Adapteva Epiphany-III
description: 16-core Parallella chip. Kickstarter-funded parallel computing for the masses. $99 supercomputer energy.

name: Kalray MPPA-256
description: 256-core French processor. VLIW clusters connected in a network-on-chip. Oui, that is a lot of cores.

name: XMOS XS1-L1
description: Event-driven multicore from Bristol. Hardware threads switch in a single cycle. Elegant, alien, underappreciated.

name: GreenArrays GA144
description: 144-core Forth chip. 18-bit cores programmed in Chuck Moore's colorForth. The most esoteric silicon ever sold.

name: Parallax Propeller P8X32A
description: 8-core hobbyist chip with its own language (Spin). No interrupts by design — each cog runs deterministically. Hackerspace royalty.

name: Ambiq Apollo4 Blue Plus
description: Ultra-low-power MCU champion. Sub-threshold voltage design sips microwatts. Your smart watch's secret weapon.

name: "Pentium 166" (Remarked P100)
description: The ink on the '166' is still wet. If you squint, you can see the original '100' underneath. Fresh from a Shenzhen back-alley workshop.

name: "Pentium II 450" (Remarked 233)
description: Slot 1 cartridge with a suspiciously hand-drawn label. Benchmarks don't lie, but the sticker sure does.

name: "Athlon XP 3200+" (Scratched 2000+)
description: IHS scratched with what appears to be a house key. The '3200+' was written in Sharpie over the sanded-off '2000+'. Bold.

name: "Core i7-4790K" (Relabeled Celeron)
description: Pop the IHS and the thermal paste hides a terrible secret. That's no i7 — that's a Celeron in a trench coat. The paste was applied extra thick for a reason.

name: "Core i9-13900K" (i3 in Disguise)
description: Retail box, holographic sticker, even a fake warranty card. Open it up and it's an i3 living its best life. The audacity is almost worth the price.

name: "Ryzen 9 5950X" (Sanded Ryzen 3)
description: Laser-engraved '5950X' on a Ryzen 3 die. Whoever did this has a steady hand and zero shame. CPU-Z tells a very different story.

name: "GTX 1060 6GB" (GTS 450 w/ Modded BIOS)
description: Device Manager says 1060. GPU-Z says GTS 450. The BIOS was modded by someone who believes in manifesting. 1GB of VRAM cosplaying as 6.

name: "RTX 3080" (GTS 450 with Dreams)
description: A GTS 450 wearing a triple-fan cooler five times its size. The heatsink alone weighs more than the hopes of whoever buys this. RGB included.

name: "RTX 4090" (Two 2060s Duct-Taped Together)
description: Two RTX 2060s in a trench coat pretending to be an RTX 4090. The duct tape is load-bearing. SLI was never meant to work like this.

name: "RX 580 8GB" (Rebadged RX 460 2GB)
description: The '8GB' sticker was placed over the '2GB' marking with surgical precision. Games crash at 1080p but it runs Minesweeper like a champ.

name: "Intel Core i5" (No Model Number)
description: Says 'Core i5' on the IHS and nothing else. Suspiciously light. Rattles when you shake it. Something is loose in there and it's not just your confidence.

name: Unknown QS Engineering Sample
description: All markings sanded off. Qualification Sample from a chip that may or may not exist. 'Fell off a truck from the fab' — the truck was going very fast.

name: Definitely Not Stolen EPYC 7742
description: 64 cores of enterprise-grade power with datacenter asset tags still attached. Serial number has been scratched off but the inventory sticker hasn't. Don't google it.

name: Suspiciously Cheap Xeon E5-2680 v4
description: Works great. Runs cool. Price was suspicious. Seller vanished after the transaction. Don't ask where it came from. Don't look at the serial number.

name: ES-0 Unreleased Prototype
description: Pre-production silicon that was never supposed to leave the lab. Somewhere, an NDA is being violated and an engineer is sweating profusely. Boots to BIOS and nothing else.

name: Recycled Pentium 4 (Landfill Edition)
description: Pulled from a landfill with pliers and optimism. Pins are slightly green with corrosion. Still posts. Planet Earth weeps but your budget rejoices.

name: Dumpster Opteron 6174
description: Found in a dumpster behind a datacenter at 2am. Smells faintly of server room and regret. 12 cores of someone else's decommissioned problem.

name: Flood Damaged Xeon E5-2690 v4
description: Survived a warehouse flood. Was placed in rice for 72 hours. Rice did not fix everything. Boots 4 out of 5 times, which is honestly pretty good.

name: Fire Sale Itanium 9560 (Scorched)
description: Literally survived a warehouse fire. The scorch marks add character. IHS has a nice patina now. It's not a bug, it's thermal seasoning.

name: Haunted Intel 486DX2-66
description: Previous owner swears it's cursed. Runs 15C hotter than spec for no reason. Makes strange clicking noises at 3am. Task Manager shows a process called 'SOUL.EXE' that can't be killed.

name: The Golden Sample (Unknown Origin)
description: One in a million. Overclocks 50%% beyond spec on air cooling. Binning perfection. The silicon gods smiled upon this die. Overclockers would commit crimes for this.

name: Bin 0 Reject (Quality Control Failure)
description: Failed every quality control test at the fab. Was supposed to be destroyed. Somehow still boots. Two cores are dead, the others are questionable. It has nothing to lose.

name: Casio fx-991ES Calculator CPU
description: 1 Hz if you mash the equals sign fast enough. Can compute sin(69) and that's about it.

name: TI-84 Plus Z80 Core
description: More games were played on this during maths class than actual maths. Drug Wars champion.

name: Sharp EL-531 Brain
description: Type 5318008 and flip it upside down. Peak computing achieved.

name: HP 48G Saturn CPU
description: RPN or die. Engineers swear by it. Normal people swear at it.

name: Brother Word Processor IC
description: It's a typewriter that thinks it's a computer. The daisy wheel disagrees.

name: Oscilloscope Prime Number Generator
description: Oscilloscope → tuned resonator → AC transistor filter network → primes only. It works. Nobody knows why. Output smells like burning.

name: Breadboard 8-Bit CPU
description: Built from a YouTube tutorial at 3am. One loose wire and it becomes a random number generator.

name: Electromagnetic Relay Computer
description: 400 relays clicking in symphony. The neighbours have filed noise complaints. Technically Turing complete.

name: Hydraulic Logic Computer
description: AND gates made from pipes and valves. OR gates from T-junctions. Runs on mains water pressure. The bathroom is now a NOR gate.

name: Domino Logic Processor
description: 10,000 dominoes arranged into a 4-bit adder. Single-use computation. Reset time: 6 hours. Worth it for the YouTube views.

name: Soldier Crab Logic Gate
description: Actual crabs in Y-shaped channels forming AND/OR gates. Peer-reviewed and published. Clock speed depends on crab motivation.

name: Minecraft Redstone CPU
description: Built in a Minecraft world. Technically runs inside a computer running inside a computer. Turtles all the way down.

name: Game of Life Turing Machine
description: A universal Turing machine built in Conway's Game of Life. Each 'clock cycle' takes 11 million generations. Be patient.

name: Antikythera Mechanism
description: 2000-year-old analogue computer for predicting eclipses. Found in a shipwreck. The OG overengineered side project.

name: Babbage Difference Engine No.2
description: Never actually finished in his lifetime. 25,000 brass parts. The original scope creep.

name: Jacquard Loom Punch Card Reader
description: The first programmable machine. Made fancy fabric. Weavers tried to destroy it. The original techno-luddite rage.

name: Faber-Castell 2/83N Slide Rule
description: They used this to get to the moon. YOUR calculator has more power and you can't even do taxes.

name: Chinese Suanpan Abacus
description: Clock speed limited by finger dexterity. Some grandmasters are faster than a calculator. No, seriously.

name: Samsung Smart Fridge SoC
description: Your fridge runs Linux now. It knows you ate that leftover pizza at 2am. It's judging you.

name: IoT Smart Toaster Controller
description: WiFi-connected toaster. Got recruited into a botnet. Now mines crypto between bread cycles.

name: Juicero Press SoC
description: $400 IoT juicer that squeezed bags you could squeeze by hand. The SoC is the most valuable thing left.

name: Bandai Tamagotchi CPU
description: Responsible for more emotional trauma than any other 4-bit processor. Your pet died because you went to school.

name: Furby 1998 CPU
description: NSA banned these from their offices. It's not really learning your language. OR IS IT. *eyes blink at 3am*

name: Tickle Me Elmo Controller
description: Caused actual riots in toy stores. More blood was shed over this chip than any GPU launch.

name: iRobot Roomba Navigation MCU
description: Maps your entire house. Definitely not sending floor plans to anyone. Bumps into chair legs existentially.

name: Nokia 3310 MAD2WD1 CPU
description: Survived being thrown at walls, dropped from buildings, and run over by cars. The CPU that refused to die. Unlike your smartphone.

name: Potato Clock Computation Node
description: 0.8V per potato. You need 47 potatoes to power a Z80. The cafeteria is getting suspicious.

name: Citrus-Powered Mining Array
description: 200 lemons wired in series to power a Raspberry Pi mining rig. Electricity cost: $0. Lemon cost: your dignity.

name: Hamster Wheel Powered Compute Node
description: 0.5W output. Uptime depends on hamster mood. Added a second hamster for redundancy. They fight.

name: RFC 1149 Pigeon Network Card
description: IP over Avian Carriers. High bandwidth, terrible latency. Packet loss increases near cats. Actually tested in real life.

name: A Rock
description: We taught rocks to think and now they have anxiety. This one hasn't learned yet. Give it 4.5 billion more years.

name: ThinkGeek USB Pet Rock
description: USB-powered rock. Plug and play. No drivers needed. No functionality either. 5-star reviews on Amazon.

name: 256-Node Raspberry Pi Cluster
description: Cost more than a real server. Slower than a real server. But look at it. LOOK AT IT. It's beautiful.

name: Google Chrome Memory Core
description: Not a CPU but it consumes more RAM than one. 47 tabs open. Each tab is a universe of wasted memory. It hungers.

name: HP Printer DRM Enforcement Chip
description: More processing power dedicated to checking ink cartridge DRM than actual printing. Refuses third-party ink with religious conviction.

name: MOS 6507 (Atari 2600)
description: The chip that started the home console wars. A cut-down 6502 with fewer address pins and a whole lot of history.

name: ANTIC Display Controller (Atari 400/800/5200)
description: Custom display coprocessor with its own instruction set. DMA-driven display lists before GPUs were cool.

name: MARIA Graphics Chip (Atari 7800)
description: Sprite-based GPU that could push 100 sprites per scanline. Too bad it launched into the post-crash wasteland.

name: General Instrument CP1610 (Intellivision)
description: A 16-bit CPU in 1979. Mattel was ahead of the curve. The overlays on the controller? Not so much.

name: Zilog Z80A (ColecoVision)
description: The ColecoVision ran on a Z80 and had the best arcade ports of its era. Donkey Kong never looked so good at home.

name: Intel 8048 (Magnavox Odyssey²)
description: First console with a real keyboard. An Intel chip in a game console before Microsoft made it mainstream.

name: Motorola 68A09 (Vectrex)
description: The only console that didn't need a TV. And nobody bought it. Vector graphics in your living room, a CRT built right in.

name: Ricoh 2A03 (NES CPU)
description: Blew into the cartridge. It didn't help but we all did it anyway. Custom 6502 with built-in sound.

name: Ricoh 2C02 (NES PPU)
description: NES Picture Processing Unit. 64 sprites, 4 palettes, and more flicker than a candle in a hurricane.

name: SA-1 Enhancement Chip (SNES)
description: A second 65816 running 3x faster than the SNES CPU, crammed into the cartridge. Super Mario RPG's secret weapon.

name: Super FX GSU-1 (SNES)
description: 3D on the SNES. Polygons so chunky you could eat them. The chip that made Star Fox possible.

name: Zilog Z80 (Sega Master System)
description: Sega's 8-bit warrior. Won Brazil, lost everywhere else. Alex Kidd was no Mario.

name: Zilog Z80 Secondary CPU (Sega Genesis)
description: An entire Z80 just for sound. Sega didn't mess around. Also provided Master System backward compatibility.

name: Hudson HuC6280 (TurboGrafx-16)
description: 16-bit marketing for an 8-bit CPU. NEC was cheeky. Modified 65C02 with extra speed and built-in I/O.

name: Zilog Z80 (Neo Geo Sound CPU)
description: Neo Geo sound CPU. Driving the Yamaha YM2610 for that legendary arcade audio. Fatal Fury never sounded so good.

name: ARM60 (3DO Interactive Multiplayer)
description: Trip Hawkins bet everything on this. He lost. $700 console with a 12.5 MHz ARM. The math didn't math.

name: Sega Saturn VDP1 (Sprite/Polygon)
description: Saturn sprite and polygon processor. Used quads instead of triangles. Made porting from PS1 a nightmare.

name: Sega Saturn VDP2 (Background/Scroll)
description: Saturn background processor. Five scrolling planes, rotation, and scaling. 2D king, 3D afterthought.

name: AMD Latte GPU (Wii U)
description: The console nobody understood. Including Nintendo. Radeon HD-class GPU powering a tablet controller nobody asked for.

name: AMD Zen 2 Custom (PS5)
description: 8 cores dedicated to loading screens being a thing of the past. RDNA2 GPU with 10.28 TFLOPS. Ray tracing on a console.

name: AMD Zen 2 Custom (Xbox Series X)
description: 12 TFLOPS in a mini-fridge form factor. The most powerful console at launch. Velocity Architecture sounds like a racing game.

name: AMD Van Gogh APU (Steam Deck)
description: Gabe Newell said 'what if Switch but PC?' and it worked. Zen 2 + RDNA2 in a handheld. Desktop Linux gaming finally arrived.

name: Sharp LR35902 (Game Boy)
description: Survived a Gulf War bombing. Tetris still running. A custom Z80-ish CPU that defined handheld gaming for a decade.

name: Sharp Z80-compatible (Game Boy Color)
description: Double the clock speed, 56 colors on screen. Pokemon Gold and Silver made this the must-have Christmas gift of '99.

name: Zilog Z80A (Sega Game Gear)
description: Sega's answer to Game Boy. Ate 6 AA batteries in 3 hours. A backlit color screen in 1990 came at a price.

name: Atari Lynx Mikey/Suzy Chipset
description: Colour handheld in 1989. Way ahead of its time. Way behind in sales. Hardware scaling and rotation before the SNES.

name: Motorola 68000 (Sega Nomad)
description: A portable Genesis. Battery life: hope. Six AA batteries for two hours of Sonic on the go.

name: Toshiba TLCS-900H (Neo Geo Pocket Color)
description: SNK's forgotten handheld. The clicky joystick was chef's kiss. Match of the Millennium deserved better.

name: ARM Cortex-A9 MPCore (PS Vita)
description: Powerful, beautiful, abandoned. The handheld equivalent of a gifted kid burnout. Quad-core ARM with PowerVR GPU.

name: ARM9 + ARM7 Dual CPU (Nintendo DS)
description: Two screens! Touch screen! Microphone! Blew into it for Zelda. ARM9 at 67MHz + ARM7 at 33MHz in one system.

name: ARM11 MPCore (Nintendo 3DS)
description: Glasses-free 3D that gave everyone headaches. Turned it off after day one. PICA200 GPU for portable polygon pushing.

name: NEC V810 (Virtual Boy)
description: Nintendo's biggest flop. Red and black only. Instant migraines. Collector gold. A 32-bit RISC CPU wasted on a headache machine.

name: NEC V30 MZ (Bandai WonderSwan)
description: Gunpei Yokoi's last console. Beautiful design, never left Japan. x86-compatible in a handheld, 40 hours on a single AA battery.

name: ARM9 (Nokia N-Gage)
description: A phone! A game console! A taco you held to your face! None done well. Sidetalkin' became a meme before memes existed.

name: Yamaha YM2149 (AY-3-8910 compatible)
description: Three square wave channels of pure 8-bit bliss. Every Spectrum game sounded like angry bees and we loved it.

name: Yamaha YM2203 OPN
description: 3 FM channels plus 3 SSG channels. The voice of the PC-88 and early Japanese arcades. J-RPG soundtracks started here.

name: Yamaha YM2413 OPLL
description: FM synthesis on a budget. Sounded like a Casio keyboard having an existential crisis.

name: Yamaha YM2151 OPM
description: The sound of the arcade. Street Fighter, OutRun, After Burner — all YM2151. Insert coin.

name: Yamaha YM3526 OPL
description: Before Sound Blaster, there was AdLib. 9 channels of FM that blew PC speaker beeps away.

name: Yamaha YM3812 OPL2
description: The sound of DOS gaming. Commander Keen, Wolfenstein, every shareware game ever. Unforgettable.

name: Yamaha YMF262 OPL3
description: OPL2's big brother. Stereo FM! Duke Nukem 3D and DOOM sounded glorious.

name: Yamaha YM2608 OPNA
description: The Japanese PC sound standard. RPGs sounded incredible. The West didn't know what they were missing.

name: Yamaha YM2610 OPNB
description: Neo Geo's voice. Fatal Fury, KOF, Metal Slug — that crisp FM + sampled drum combo.

name: Yamaha DX7 OPS (YM21280)
description: THE sound of the 1980s. Every pop song, every movie soundtrack, every elevator. This chip defined a decade.

name: Yamaha YMF724 DS-1
description: The cheap PCI sound card your prebuilt PC came with. It worked. That's the nicest thing anyone can say.

name: Yamaha YMZ280B
description: ADPCM playback workhorse of mid-90s arcade boards. 8 channels of sampled audio. The voice behind a thousand coin-ops.

name: Roland MT-32 LA Synthesis Engine
description: The REAL way to play Sierra and LucasArts adventure games. Monkey Island sounded like a movie with this.

name: Roland SC-55 Sound Canvas Engine
description: The General MIDI gold standard. DOOM's MIDI soundtrack on this was *chef's kiss*.

name: Roland D-50 LA Chip
description: Enya's entire career runs on this chip. Orinoco Flow is just D-50 presets.

name: Roland TB-303 CPU
description: Designed for guitarists to practice with. Nobody bought it. Then DJs found it and invented an entire genre. THE accident that changed music.

name: Roland TR-808 Voice Chips
description: Kanye West, Marvin Gaye, Phil Collins — all 808. The most influential drums ever made from a 'failed' product.

name: Roland TR-909 Voice Chips
description: If 808 made hip-hop, 909 made techno. Every house and techno track since 1985 owes this chip a royalty.

name: Roland BOSS DSP
description: Inside every BOSS pedal on every pedalboard on every stage. The unsung hero of live music.

name: MOS 8580 SID
description: Less distortion, more filter precision. SID purists argue about 6581 vs 8580 like vinyl vs digital.

name: Ensoniq ES1370 AudioPCI
description: The cheap Sound Blaster alternative. Came free with your Compaq Presario.

name: Ensoniq ES1371 / Creative CT5880
description: Creative bought Ensoniq and slapped their name on it. Circle of life.

name: General Instrument AY-3-8910
description: In the Vectrex, Intellivision, MSX, ZX Spectrum 128, Atari ST... this chip got around.

name: General Instrument AY-3-8912
description: Same chip, fewer pins. For when 40 pins was too many pins.

name: General Instrument SP0256
description: HELLO I AM THE COMPUTER. The robotic voice of 80s toys and arcade games. DESTROY ALL HUMANS.

name: TI SN76489
description: Three tone channels and a noise channel. Simple. Effective. In literally everything.

name: TI SN76477
description: Pew pew pew. Seriously, this chip made THE Space Invaders sounds. Also used in pinball machines.

name: Philips SAA1099
description: 6 channels of stereo square waves. The Sam Coupé's one claim to fame.

name: Atari POKEY
description: Four channels of audio plus keyboard scanning plus random number generation. Multitasking before it was cool.

name: Atari TIA
description: Two channels of audio. Beeps and boops. But those beeps and boops started an industry.

name: Creative CT1350 DSP (Sound Blaster 1.0)
description: The card that killed AdLib. Digitised audio on a PC! You could record your own voice! Revolutionary.

name: Creative CT1745 DSP (Sound Blaster 16)
description: SB16 compatible? Compatible with EVERYTHING. The sound card standard for a decade.

name: Creative EMU10K1 (Sound Blaster Live!)
description: Hardware EAX! Environmental audio! Quake in a cave actually sounded like a cave.

name: Creative EMU10K2 (Audigy)
description: 24-bit! 100dB SNR! Mostly used to play MP3s and Counter-Strike.

name: Creative X-Fi Xtreme Audio CA20K1
description: The final boss of sound cards before motherboard audio made them irrelevant. Pour one out.

name: Konami SCC
description: Konami put custom sound chips IN THE GAME CARTRIDGES. Gradius, Snatcher, Salamander — absolute bangers.

name: Ricoh RF5C68
description: Gave the Sega CD its sampled audio. Red Book CD audio AND this chip? Luxury.

name: Namco C30 WSG
description: Wakka wakka wakka. The most recognisable sound in gaming history came from this chip.

name: Nintendo S-SMP / S-DSP (SNES)
description: Designed by Sony (yes, THAT Sony). 8 channels of ADPCM. DKC, FF6, Chrono Trigger — some of the best game music ever.

name: Game Boy APU (Sharp LR35902)
description: 4 channels. Two pulse waves, one wave, one noise. Somehow produced the Pokémon battle theme.

name: Capcom CPS-2 Q-Sound DSP
description: Stereo positioning in a mono speaker arcade cab. Capcom was doing spatial audio before it was cool.

name: Hudson HuC6280 PSG
description: 6 wavetable channels. PC Engine games sounded surprisingly good for a console nobody in the West owned.

name: Sunsoft 5B (Yamaha YM2149 variant)
description: Sunsoft put an extra sound chip in an NES cartridge for ONE GAME. And that game's soundtrack SLAPS.

name: OKI MSM6295
description: The voice chip of the arcade. FINISH HIM. HADOUKEN. TOASTY! All OKI.

name: Twisted Electrons AY3 Module
description: The 1978 PSG chip reborn as a Eurorack module. Chiptune in your modular rig.

name: MOS SID 6582 ARMSID
description: Your C64's SID died? This FPGA drop-in replacement is arguably better. SID purists are furious.

name: Apollo Guidance Computer (AGC)
description: Landed humans on the Moon with less computing power than your thermostat. Margaret Hamilton's software saved Apollo 11 when this thing threw a 1202 alarm.

name: IBM System/4Pi AP-101S
description: Five of these ran the Space Shuttle. Voted on every decision. If three agreed, majority ruled. Democracy at Mach 25.

name: Gemini Guidance Computer
description: 39-bit word length because NASA didn't do things by halves. Helped two spacecraft dock for the first time. No pressure.

name: Voyager AACS (RCA 1802 COSMAC)
description: Still running. In interstellar space. Since 1977. On 69KB of memory. The most reliable computer ever built. It will outlive us all.

name: Cassini-Huygens RAD6000
description: Orbited Saturn for 13 years. Photographed its rings, moons, and storms. Then dove into the atmosphere on purpose. What a way to go.

name: New Horizons Mongoose-V
description: Flew past Pluto. Took THE photo. Running MIPS R3000 architecture at the edge of the solar system. Light-speed round trip: 10 hours.

name: BAE RAD750
description: Powers Curiosity, Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter, and more. $200,000 per chip. Laughs at cosmic rays that would kill your i9.

name: Perseverance RAD750
description: Same chip, different planet. Controls a nuclear-powered rover AND a helicopter on Mars. Your laptop can't even run Chrome properly.

name: Ingenuity Qualcomm Snapdragon 801
description: A phone chip flying a helicopter on Mars. Yes, really. Same SoC as a 2014 smartphone. First powered flight on another planet.

name: Mars Pathfinder / Sojourner RAD6000
description: First rover on Mars. Ran VxWorks RTOS. Had a priority inversion bug that nearly killed the mission. Fixed from 190 million km away.

name: Spirit/Opportunity RAD6000
description: Opportunity was supposed to last 90 days. Lasted 15 YEARS. The little rover that could. 'My battery is low and it's getting dark.'

name: Hubble Space Telescope DF-224
description: Launched with a dodgy mirror but still revolutionised astronomy. Replaced with a 486 in 1999. The Hubble runs on a 486. Let that sink in.

name: Hubble 486 Upgrade
description: 25x faster than what it replaced. Astronauts flew to orbit to install a 486 into a telescope. The most expensive IT upgrade in history.

name: James Webb Space Telescope RAD750
description: Looking at the first galaxies ever formed. 1.5 million km from Earth. $10 billion telescope. Runs on a chip from 2001. Works flawlessly.

name: GPS III Satellite Processor
description: Knows where you are within 30cm. 31 satellites, 20,200km up, doing orbital math so your phone can find the nearest kebab shop.

name: ISS Multiplexer/Demultiplexer MDM
description: Keeps the International Space Station running. Controls life support, thermal management, and communications. No blue screens allowed.

name: MIL-STD-1750A
description: The US military's standard instruction set for 20 years. In every jet, missile, and satellite. Classified performance charts.

name: F-22 Raptor Common Integrated Processor (CIP)
description: Processes sensor fusion for the world's most advanced fighter jet. Slower than your phone. Faster than you'll ever need at Mach 2.

name: F-35 Integrated Core Processor (ICP)
description: The F-35's brain. Processes more sensor data than a small city's internet traffic. Budget overruns not included.

name: JDAM GPS/INS Guidance Processor
description: Turns a dumb bomb into a smart bomb. $25,000 GPS kit. Accurate to 5 metres. The processor with the most impactful single-use job.

name: Tomahawk DSMAC Processor
description: Digital Scene Matching. The missile takes photos of the ground and compares them to a stored map. Image recognition before AI was cool.

name: Patriot Missile Guidance Computer
description: A floating-point rounding error in this system caused a 0.34 second timing drift that missed a Scud missile in 1991. Bugs have consequences.

name: Minuteman I/II Guidance Computer D-37C
description: First mass-produced integrated circuits were bought for THIS. Nuclear missile guidance. The military funded the entire IC revolution.

name: Xilinx Virtex-5QV (Rad-Hard FPGA)
description: Space-grade FPGA. Survives 1 Mrad of radiation. You need to be a government contractor to buy one. No, seriously.

name: Cobham GR740 LEON4
description: European space-grade quad-core. SPARC architecture. ESA's go-to processor. Because Europe wanted their own space chips.

name: Vorago VA10820
description: ARM Cortex-M0 that survives -55°C to +200°C and handles radiation. For when your MCU needs to work in literal hell.

name: AN/UYK-43
description: The US Navy's standard shipboard computer for 30 years. On every submarine, carrier, and destroyer. Waterproof is an understatement.

name: AN/UYK-44
description: Miniaturised navy computer. Small enough for a torpedo. Yes, torpedoes have computers. No, they don't run Windows.

name: NSA Type 1 Crypto Processor
description: All specs classified. This chip handles encryption for TOP SECRET communications. We could tell you more but... you know.

name: Clipper Chip MYK-78
description: The government wanted a backdoor in every phone. The public said no. The first great crypto war. The chip that launched a thousand protests.

name: Enigma Rotor Mechanism
description: Not a chip, but the encryption 'processor' that took Bletchley Park and Alan Turing to crack. The most consequential cipher machine in history.

name: Colossus Thyratron Tube
description: The world's first programmable electronic computer. Built to crack Lorenz ciphers. Classified until the 1970s. Tommy Flowers paid for parts out of his own pocket.

name: Hamilton Pulsar Module
description: The first digital watch. Cost $2,100 in 1972. James Bond wore one. You pressed a button to see the time because the LED drained the battery.

name: Seiko 06LC Module
description: First LCD digital watch. Seiko went LCD while everyone else was doing LED. Seiko was right.

name: Texas Instruments Digital Watch Module
description: TI crashed the price of digital watches from $2000 to $20. Destroyed the Swiss watch industry overnight. Casio took notes.

name: Casio F-91W Module 593
description: The most popular watch ever made. $10. Battery lasts 7 years. Worn by presidents and terrorists alike. The great equaliser.

name: Casio G-Shock DW-5000C Module
description: Kikuo Ibe dropped his father's watch and vowed to make an unbreakable one. He did. Survived a 10m drop onto concrete.

name: Casio CA-53W Calculator Watch Module
description: Calculator on your wrist. Walter White wore one. Every nerd in the 80s had one. The ultimate power move in maths class.

name: Casio CFX-400 Scientific Calculator Watch
description: Scientific calculator AND a watch. Could do trig on your wrist. Absolutely nobody needed this. Absolutely everybody wanted it.

name: Casio WMP-1V MP3 Watch
description: An MP3 player watch in 2000. 32MB storage. About 8 songs. The future arrived early but it was very small.

name: Casio CMD-40 TV Remote Watch
description: Controlled any TV from your wrist. Every kid's dream. Every teacher's nightmare. Banned in some classrooms.

name: Seiko UC-2000 Wrist Computer
description: A wrist computer with a tiny keyboard dock. You could write BASIC programs on your arm. Peak 80s cyberpunk.

name: Seiko Ruputer/OnHand PC
description: The first smartwatch. Ran a real OS. Had apps. In 1998. Seiko was 15 years early and nobody cared.

name: Seiko TV Watch T001
description: A TV on your wrist. James Bond wore this one too. Tiny screen, tinier picture. Absolutely magnificent nonsense.

name: Microsoft SPOT Watch
description: Microsoft's smartwatch. Used FM radio for data. Required a subscription. Lasted 4 years. Peak Microsoft energy.

name: Pebble Original STM32F205
description: Kickstarter darling. E-paper display. Week-long battery. The people's smartwatch before Fitbit killed it and Apple danced on the grave.

name: Pebble Time STM32F439
description: Colour e-paper! Timeline UI! Community loved it! Fitbit bought it and killed everything! We're not bitter! (We're bitter.)

name: Samsung Galaxy Gear Exynos
description: Samsung's first smartwatch. Camera on the strap. Looked ridiculous. Samsung threw everything at the wall to see what stuck.

name: Apple Watch S1 SiP
description: The watch that made smartwatches mainstream. Also the watch that made people tap their wrists and look insane in public.

name: Apple Watch S9 SiP
description: More powerful than most laptops from 2010. On your wrist. To tell you to stand up and breathe. $400 nag machine.

name: Samsung Exynos W930
description: Dual-core Cortex-A55 on your wrist. Runs Wear OS. Google and Samsung's awkward partnership in chip form.

name: Google Pixel Watch Exynos 9110
description: Google's first watch used Samsung's old chip from 2018. Four years old at launch. Classic Google.

name: Fitbit Charge HR STM32L151
description: Counted your steps. Judged your sleep. Made you competitive with coworkers. Destroyed friendships over weekend step challenges.

name: Garmin Forerunner 235
description: Every runner's gateway drug into GPS watches. Next thing you know you're buying chest straps and arguing about VO2 max.

name: Xiaomi Mi Band 3 Dialog DA14681
description: $25 fitness tracker. 20-day battery. Outsold everything. Proof that 'good enough and cheap' beats 'premium and pricey' every time.

name: Garmin Fenix 7
description: The watch for ultramarathoners and people who wish they were ultramarathoners. Solar charging. Topo maps. Built like a tank.

name: Nike+ FuelBand MCU
description: Nike's fitness band. Invented 'NikeFuel' points that meant nothing. Discontinued in 2014. Tim Cook was on Nike's board the whole time.

name: Google Glass OMAP4430
description: OK Glass, why is everyone calling me a Glasshole? The most socially rejected gadget since the Bluetooth earpiece.

name: Nreal/XREAL Air AR Glasses SoC
description: AR glasses that actually look normal-ish. Humanity's second attempt at face computers. This time with less public shaming.

name: Tamagotchi Connection IC
description: The Tamagotchi came back. With infrared. You could connect with friends. Your digital pet still died when you went to school.

name: Tiger Game.com Processor
description: Touch screen handheld in 1997! Internet capable! Sounded incredible! Played terribly! Every game was a slideshow!

name: Cybiko Wireless Computer
description: Wireless handheld computer for teens. Had its own mesh network. Could message friends in class. Basically Discord in 2000.

name: LED Throwies Controller
description: LED + battery + magnet. Not a computer. Throw it at metal things and it glows. Called 'wearable tech' on exactly one grant application.

name: Nixie Tube Watch Controller
description: A watch with actual Nixie tubes. Warm orange glow. Steampunk approved. Battery lasts about as long as your patience explaining what Nixie tubes are.

name: Pip-Boy 3000 Replica Controller
description: The Fallout 4 collector's edition Pip-Boy. Held your phone. Made of cheap plastic. Worth it for the vibes. War never changes.

name: Rockwell 11C11 Modem Chipset
description: The chip behind every BBS connection. 300/1200 baud. Every screech and handshake started here. The gateway drug to online life.

name: US Robotics Sportster 14.4 (TI DSP)
description: 14.4 kbps. Downloading a single MP3 took 45 minutes. And you were grateful.

name: US Robotics Courier V.Everything HST DSP
description: The Rolls Royce of modems. Sysops swore by the Courier. If you ran a BBS, this was the status symbol.

name: Conexant/Rockwell HSP56 Softmodem
description: 56K! The sound. You know the sound. KKKKKKRRRRSHHHHHHH DING DING DING BONG BONG SHHHHHHH. Connected. Welcome to the internet.

name: Lucent/Agere WinModem DSP
description: Software modem. Used your CPU for processing. Linux users fought driver battles for YEARS. The modem that launched a thousand forum posts.

name: Hayes Smartmodem Controller
description: Invented the AT command set. Every modem since speaks Hayes. ATDT was the 'www.' of the 1980s. OK.

name: Alcatel/Thomson SpeedTouch ADSL Chipset
description: Always-on internet! No more dial-up! Your phone works AND you're online! Broadband was black magic in 2000.

name: Broadcom BCM6348 ADSL2+ SoC
description: Inside every ISP-provided router your parents never rebooted. The unsung hero of early broadband. Try turning it off and on again.

name: Globespan Semiconductor ADSL Chipset
description: One of the first ADSL chipsets. 1.5 Mbps felt like teleportation after 56K. Downloaded Napster in minutes instead of hours.

name: Intel 82586 Ethernet Coprocessor
description: Early Ethernet coprocessor. 10 Mbps on thick yellow coax cable. Networking when cables were literally the size of garden hoses.

name: DEC DC21040 Tulip Ethernet Controller
description: The Tulip. Every Linux admin's favourite NIC. Open documentation. Just worked. The people's Ethernet controller.

name: 3Com 3C905B-TX EtherLink XL
description: THE network card of the late 90s. Every office PC, every LAN party. If it wasn't a 3Com, it was a Realtek. No in-between.

name: Realtek RTL8139 Fast Ethernet
description: The cheapest NIC ever. $3. In every beige PC worldwide. Drivers were questionable. Performance was questionable. Price was unbeatable.

name: Realtek RTL8111 Gigabit Ethernet
description: Gigabit Ethernet for the masses. On every motherboard for 20 years. Still going. The cockroach of networking chips.

name: Intel I210 Gigabit Ethernet Controller
description: The server-grade NIC. When Realtek wasn't good enough and you needed actual reliability. Sysadmins trust Intel NICs with their lives.

name: Intel I225-V 2.5GbE Controller
description: 2.5 Gigabit! Progress! Also shipped with a firmware bug that caused random disconnects. Intel giveth and Intel taketh away.

name: Intel 82574L Gigabit Ethernet
description: The pfSense darling. Every DIY router and firewall ran on boards with this chip. If you know what pfSense is, you've used this.

name: Lucent/Agere WaveLAN ORiNOCO
description: One of the first WiFi cards. PCMCIA slot. 2 Mbps. 802.11b. Wardrivers loved these. NetStumbler + ORiNOCO + Pringles cantenna = 2002.

name: Atheros AR5212 802.11a/b/g
description: The WiFi hacker's best friend. Monitor mode. Packet injection. Aircrack-ng worked perfectly. The penetration tester's weapon of choice.

name: Atheros AR9280 802.11n Dual-Band
description: 802.11n dual-band. Open-source ath9k driver. Linux loved it. The gold standard for open-source WiFi. Still works perfectly.

name: Intel WiFi Link 5300 802.11n
description: 802.11n 3x3 MIMO. In every ThinkPad. Intel whitelist locked so you couldn't replace it with anything else. Thanks, Lenovo.

name: Broadcom BCM4311 802.11b/g
description: In every laptop. No Linux drivers. Broadcom said 'no.' Community reverse-engineered them anyway. The WiFi chip that radicalised Linux users.

name: Broadcom BCM43602 802.11ac
description: 802.11ac. Apple used these in MacBooks. Broadcom eventually open-sourced drivers. Only took a decade. Progress.

name: Qualcomm Atheros QCA6174 802.11ac
description: 802.11ac MU-MIMO. In a million laptops. The chip that made WiFi actually reliable enough to mostly trust for video calls.

name: Intel AX200 WiFi 6
description: WiFi 6. OFDMA. 160MHz channels. Big numbers. Your router probably doesn't support half of it. But the chip does.

name: Intel AX211 WiFi 6E
description: WiFi 6E. 6GHz band. So much spectrum. So few devices that support it. Living in the future, alone.

name: Qualcomm FastConnect 7800 WiFi 7
description: WiFi 7. MLO. 320MHz channels. 5.8 Gbps theoretical. Your ISP is the bottleneck now, not your WiFi. How the tables have turned.

name: Realtek RTL8812AU 802.11ac USB
description: The Kali Linux USB WiFi adapter chip. Supports monitor mode. Every cybersecurity student's Amazon purchase history has one.

name: Broadcom BCM5695 StrataXGS II
description: Inside managed switches worldwide. Broadcom owns the switching silicon market. If you've used the internet, packets hit a Broadcom chip.

name: Broadcom BCM56960 Tomahawk
description: 3.2 Tbps switching capacity. In every major datacenter. Netflix, AWS, Google — all running through Tomahawks. The internet's backbone chip.

name: Broadcom BCM56990 Tomahawk 4
description: 25.6 Tbps. One chip moves more data than entire countries' internet traffic. The absolute unit of switching silicon.

name: Cisco QuantumFlow Network Processor
description: Cisco's custom network processor. 40 cores. Powers the ASR routers that route the actual internet. BGP's best friend.

name: Cisco Silicon One Q100
description: Cisco finally made one chip for routing AND switching. Only took them 30 years to figure that out.

name: Marvell Prestera 98DX3236
description: In every small managed switch. Ubiquiti, MikroTik, TP-Link — Marvell Prestera inside. The affordable networking workhorse.

name: Barefoot Networks / Intel Tofino
description: P4 programmable switch. You can reprogram the forwarding plane. Network engineers either love it or fear it. There is no middle ground.

name: Mellanox ConnectX-6 DX
description: 200 Gbps InfiniBand/Ethernet. Powers every HPC cluster and AI training farm. GPT was trained through cards with this chip. The unsung AI enabler.

name: CSR BlueCore2 Bluetooth Controller
description: In every cheap Bluetooth dongle. The $1 USB Bluetooth adapter that kinda worked. Pair, unpair, pair again, throw out window, buy new one.

name: Nordic nRF51822 BLE SoC
description: BLE on a chip. In every fitness tracker, beacon, and smart device. If it's Bluetooth Low Energy, there's probably a Nordic chip inside.

name: Espressif ESP8266 WiFi MCU
description: $2 WiFi chip that started the IoT revolution. Arduino hackers went feral. Suddenly everything was connected. Your plant pot tweets now.

name: Texas Instruments CC2531 Zigbee SoC
description: Zigbee coordinator in a $5 USB stick. Home Assistant users buy these in bulk. Smart home on a budget. IKEA Tradfri runs on these.

name: NE2000 Clone (UMC 9008) Ethernet
description: THE network card standard. Everything was 'NE2000 compatible.' IRQ conflicts were a way of life. IRQ5, DMA3, base 300h — the sacred numbers.

name: Western Digital WD8003 Ethernet
description: One of the first Ethernet cards for PC. Token Ring was the 'enterprise' choice. Ethernet won anyway. Simplicity beats committees.

name: Intel 82596 LAN Coprocessor
description: 32-bit Ethernet coprocessor. Handled TCP/IP offloading before it was trendy. In Sun workstations and high-end servers.

name: ARCnet COM9026 Controller
description: Before Ethernet. 2.5 Mbps token-passing network. Dentist offices used ARCnet well into the 2000s. Some say they still do.

name: TI TMS380C16 Token Ring Controller
description: IBM's pet networking standard. Token passing. Deterministic. Expensive. Ethernet killed it by being cheap and good enough. The VHS vs Betamax of networking.

name: GeForce RTX 5090 D
description: The dragon-hoarding cousin of the RTX 5090, restricted for certain markets.

name: RTX PRO 5000 72GB Blackwell
description: A professional Blackwell monster with enough VRAM to hallucinate the entire internet.

name: GeForce RTX 5080
description: Blackwell's second-in-command, ready to render your regrets in real-time ray tracing.

name: RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Workstation Edition
description: When your workstation needs to out-render your imagination.

name: GeForce RTX 4080
description: Ada Lovelace's flagship mid-tier that costs more than some used cars.

name: GeForce RTX 4080 SUPER
description: The SUPER designation is Nvidia's way of saying 'we held back last time, whoops'.

name: RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell
description: Blackwell professional muscle for CAD jockeys and simulation wizards.

name: GeForce RTX 5070 Ti
description: The Ti badge returns with a vengeance on Blackwell's second tier.

name: GeForce RTX 4070 Ti SUPER
description: Ada's most confusingly named GPU, but undeniably excellent.

name: RTX PRO 5000 Blackwell
description: Professional Blackwell for those who expense their hardware.

name: GeForce RTX 4070 Ti
description: The 4080 12GB that Nvidia renamed after community outrage. It stuck.

name: GeForce RTX 5090 D v2
description: The sequel nobody asked for but certain markets required.

name: GeForce RTX 4090 D
description: Ada Lovelace's export-restricted sibling with a slightly smaller ego.

name: RTX 5000 Ada Generation
description: Ada workstation power without the gaming RGB frills.

name: GeForce RTX 4070 SUPER
description: More cores, same price tier  -- Ada's SUPER refresh hits the sweet spot.

name: GeForce RTX 3090 Ti
description: Ampere's last gasp  -- the Ti that ate the 4090's launch lunch.

name: RTX PRO 4000 Blackwell
description: Mid-range Blackwell pro for the serious but not extravagant professional.

name: Radeon RX 7900 XT
description: RDNA3's nearly-flagship for those who found the XTX price tag alarming.

name: GeForce RTX 5070
description: Blackwell's mid-range champ, punching well above its price class.

name: RTX 6000 Ada Generation
description: The fully unlocked Ada die  -- for professionals with expense accounts to match.

name: GeForce RTX 5090 Laptop GPU
description: A desktop flagship crammed into a chassis that voids your lap's warranty.

name: RTX 4500 Ada Generation
description: Ada workstation mid-range  -- ECC memory and no game benchmarks needed.

name: Radeon RX 6950 XT
description: RDNA2's final form  -- a refresh that squeezed every last MHz out of Navi 21.

name: Radeon PRO W7900
description: AMD's RDNA3 workstation flagship with 48GB of professional-grade memory.

name: RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition
description: Blackwell in a server suit  -- it doesn't need RGB when it has ROI.

name: Radeon PRO W7800
description: 32GB of professional RDNA3 muscle for the visualization crowd.

name: Radeon AI PRO R9700
description: AMD's RDNA4-based AI-focused pro card for the inference-at-the-edge crowd.

name: nVidia L40
description: Ada datacenter workhorse  -- no display outputs, no regrets.

name: Radeon RX 7900 GRE
description: Golden Rabbit Edition  -- RDNA3 for markets that appreciate a good deal.

name: GeForce RTX 4090 Laptop GPU
description: The full Ada experience in a chassis that makes cooling engineers sweat.

name: GeForce RTX 4070
description: Ada's mainstream hero  -- DLSS 3 and frame gen for the masses.

name: Radeon RX 9070 XT
description: RDNA4's flagship mainstream card, making Nvidia sweat at the $600 price point.

name: GeForce RTX 3080 Ti
description: Ampere's 90-class performance at a 80-class price. Almost.

name: GeForce RTX 3080 12GB
description: More memory than you asked for, same thermals you feared.

name: GeForce RTX 5080 Laptop GPU
description: Blackwell's second-best stuffed into a gaming laptop near you.

name: Radeon RX 9070
description: RDNA4 non-XT  -- still a killer deal at its launch price.

name: RTX 5880 Ada Generation
description: The workstation Ada that slipped in quietly between product generations.

name: Radeon RX 6800 XT
description: RDNA2's near-flagship  -- the card that put AMD back in the high-end conversation.

name: GeForce RTX 3080
description: Turns electricity directly into heat and imaginary coins; may cause spontaneous PSU existential crises.

name: GeForce RTX 4080 Laptop GPU
description: Ada's second-tier shrunk down for mobile workstations and gaming laptops.

name: Radeon RX 9070 GRE
description: Another Golden Rabbit Edition  -- RDNA4 for the budget-conscious enthusiast.

name: Radeon RX 7800 XT
description: RDNA3's 1080p/1440p champ with enough VRAM to actually handle modern textures.

name: RTX 4000 Ada Generation

name: RTX A5000
description: Ampere workstation muscle with 24GB of ECC memory and zero gaming guilt.

name: RTX A6000

name: GeForce RTX 5060 Ti 16GB
description: Blackwell mid-range with the VRAM buffer everyone wanted years ago.

name: GeForce RTX 4060 Ti 16GB
description: For those who refused to buy the 8GB version on principle.

name: Radeon RX 7700 XT
description: RDNA3 mid-range with a chiplet design that runs surprisingly cool.

name: GeForce RTX 4060 Ti
description: Efficient Ada mid-ranger that sparked a thousand bandwidth arguments.

name: GeForce RTX 5060 Ti 8GB
description: Same Blackwell chip, half the VRAM  -- for those who game at 1080p forever.

name: GeForce RTX 3070
description: All the hashrate of a 2080 Ti without the prestige; perfect for a mid-tier junkyard rig.

name: Radeon RX 7900M

name: Radeon RX 6800
description: RDNA2's cut-down flagship  -- still a monster at 1440p and beyond.

name: NVIDIA A10
description: Datacenter Ampere built for inference  -- single-slot, single-purpose, seriously fast.

name: Radeon RX 7700
description: RDNA3 value tier  -- not quite 7700 XT but still punchy at 1080p/1440p.

name: RTX A5500
description: Mobile workstation Ampere with 16GB of ECC memory and laptop GPU diplomacy.

name: GeForce RTX 5060
description: Blackwell's budget entry  -- for 1080p gaming in the mid-2020s.

name: RTX A4500
description: Workstation Ampere with 20GB ECC  -- your CAD software will be very happy.

name: GeForce RTX 3070 Ti
description: The Ti that added GDDR6X and a few hundred MHz  -- and a few extra watts.

name: RTX PRO 2000 Blackwell
description: Entry-level Blackwell pro  -- because even accountants need GPU compute now.

name: Radeon PRO W7700
description: RDNA3 workstation for creative professionals who prefer red team.

name: GeForce RTX 5070 Ti Laptop GPU

name: Radeon RX 6750 XT

name: GeForce RTX 3060 Ti
description: Silicon sweet spot; miners loved it, gamers hated that miners loved it.

name: Radeon PRO W6800
description: 32GB RDNA2 workstation  -- the creative professional's dream in red.

name: Radeon RX 9060 XT 16GB
description: RDNA4 mainstream with plenty of VRAM for the texture-heavy future ahead.

name: L40S
description: Ada datacenter S-class  -- Ada Lovelace optimized for AI inference at scale.

name: TITAN RTX
description: Turing's golden child  -- 24GB VRAM before it was cool.

name: Quadro RTX 8000
description: 48GB of Turing  -- for professionals who wouldn't blink at the five-figure price tag.

name: TITAN V
description: Volta-powered research card; horrifically expensive, suspiciously comfortable living in a dusty lab rack.

name: Radeon RX 9060 XT 8GB
description: RDNA4 mainstream without the VRAM premium  -- great for 1080p gaming.

name: Radeon RX 6700 XT
description: RDNA2 mid-range debut with 12GB VRAM and AMD Infinity Cache magic.

name: GeForce RTX 4070 Laptop GPU
description: Ada mobile mid-range  -- solid 1080p/1440p performance in a backpackable form.

name: GeForce RTX 4060
description: Ada's mainstream pick  -- efficient, capable, and just barely enough VRAM.

name: GeForce RTX 2080 SUPER
description: The Turing refresh that made the original 2080 buyers reach for their receipts.

name: RTX A4000
description: Single-slot Ampere workstation  -- your neighbors' PCIe slots will thank you.

name: GeForce RTX 5070 Laptop GPU
description: Blackwell mid-range in laptop trim  -- more than enough for portable gaming glory.

name: GeForce RTX 3080 Ti Laptop GPU
description: Ampere's laptop Ti flagship  -- a desktop 3080's performance in a slab of aluminium.

name: Radeon RX 6700
description: Cut-down Navi 22 for the mainstream  -- 10GB VRAM and good 1080p legs.

name: NVIDIA TITAN Xp
description: Pascal's ultimate consumer card  -- named with an XP for extra extreme pizzazz.

name: GeForce RTX 2080
description: Turing's mainstream flagship  -- ray tracing existed, but no one could run it yet.

name: GeForce GTX 1080 Ti
description: Pascal's magnum opus  -- so good it embarrassed the GTX 1080's launch buyers.

name: Radeon RX 7650 GRE
description: RDNA3 budget entry  -- the Golden Rabbit Edition for value hunters.

name: Radeon RX 9060
description: RDNA4 budget card  -- entry-level never looked so next-gen.

name: GeForce RTX 2070 SUPER
description: SUPER refreshes were Nvidia saying 'our bad' with better specs at the same price.

name: Radeon 8060S
description: Integrated RDNA4 muscle in a laptop APU that makes discrete GPUs nervous.

name: Quadro RTX 6000
description: 24GB Turing for workstations  -- the card that made render farms swoon.

name: GeForce RTX 3070 Ti Laptop GPU
description: Ampere Ti in laptop form  -- for those who game on the go at 1440p.

name: GeForce RTX 4060 Laptop GPU
description: Ada mobile mainstream  -- efficient enough to almost justify the battery drain.

name: GeForce RTX 5050
description: Blackwell's entry-level  -- DLSS 4 and Multi Frame Gen even for budget buyers.

name: Radeon RX 7600 XT
description: 16GB of RDNA3 for the VRAM-anxious budget gamer.

name: Radeon RX 6650 XT
